items.question: Who introduced the KL divergence measure?
items.answer: Solomon Kullback and Richard Leibler introduced the directed information quantity in 1951, building on Shannon's entropy; it is now standard in ML as D_KL(P || Q).

items.question: What is the definition of D_KL(P || Q) for discrete distributions?
items.answer: D_KL(P || Q) = Σ_x p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)) = E_P[log(p(X)/q(X))], the expected log-likelihood ratio when sampling X from P.

items.question: When does D_KL(P || Q) equal zero?
items.answer: D_KL(P || Q) = 0 if and only if P and Q are the same distribution (identical PMF over the shared support), by Gibbs' inequality.

items.question: Where does KL appear in classification training?
items.answer: Cross-entropy loss H(P, Q) decomposes as H(P) + D_KL(P || Q); for fixed one-hot labels H(P) is constant, so minimizing cross-entropy minimizes KL from labels to model predictions.

items.question: Why is KL divergence not a symmetric distance?
items.answer: The expectation is taken under P for D_KL(P || Q) and under Q for D_KL(Q || P), weighting outcomes differently; asymmetry reflects directed 'extra surprise' when Q approximates P, not geometric distance.

items.question: How do you compute KL from cross-entropy and entropy?
items.answer: Subtract: D_KL(P || Q) = H(P, Q) − H(P). Compute H(P, Q) = −Σ p log q and H(P) = −Σ p log p on the same support, using consistent log base.

items.question: How does D_KL(P || Q) differ from D_KL(Q || P)?
items.answer: They use different sampling distributions: forward KL averages log(p/q) under P (mode-seeking fit of Q); reverse KL averages log(q/p) under Q (mean-seeking, penalizes Q mass where P is small). Values differ unless P = Q.

items.question: KL divergence vs Jensen–Shannon divergence — when to use which?
items.answer: KL is directed and can be infinite on support mismatch; JS symmetrizes via mixture M = (P+Q)/2 and is bounded, better for symmetric comparison. Use KL when the objective is inherently directed (MLE, ELBO terms); use JS for balanced similarity.

items.question: What if q(x) = 0 for some x where p(x) > 0?
items.answer: The term p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)) is infinite, so D_KL(P || Q) = ∞. Optimization must enforce support coverage (e.g. full softmax over classes) or smooth q before KL is meaningful.

items.question: What if P and Q are identical except for tiny probability shifts on a large vocabulary?
items.answer: KL can still be non-trivial: many small log-ratio contributions accumulate. In language models with 50K tokens, even per-token shifts of 0.001 can sum to sizable total KL across the vocabulary.
